---
title: "Florida Rayless Goldenrod (Bigelowia australis)"
slug: "bigelowia-australis"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476410870/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Florida Rayless Goldenrod"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476410870/medium.jpg" alt="Florida Rayless Goldenrod" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lmcconachie">
lmcconachie</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/265248466">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=1600605)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small, shrubby wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and clusters of yellow flower heads that lack showy ray petals. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, sandhills, and open pine flatwoods, particularly in central regions. It typically blooms during the cooler to warmer months and contributes to the diversity of native groundcover in fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1600605-Bigelowia-australis)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/1207)
